# Questions in Livestream 142

Live Stream #142: Think About the Questions!!!

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=447" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:27</a> | Has later research materially changed the Egyptian loanword argument for the historicity of the Exodus? | He says no. New work has added detail, but it has not materially altered the overall argument. | He says the argument has not shifted because lexicography and peer-reviewed publication move slowly. More Hebrew and Egyptian data have appeared, including additional possible loanwords, but he treats that as strengthening the existing case rather than undermining it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:44</a> | What is the gift of tongues in 1 Corinthians 14, and are they angelic tongues? | He treats tongues as real language, not gibberish; in Second Temple thought that could include angelic language, while "mysteries" are speech not understood by ordinary hearers. | He reads 1 Corinthians 14 as referring to coherent systems of communication, whether human languages or, in Second Temple categories, languages of heavenly beings. The point of "mysteries" is not nonsense but private or concealed religious experience, which is why uninterpreted tongues edify only the speaker while prophecy edifies the church. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=1207" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:07</a> | Is the sin of gluttony still relevant when food is not rationed or scarce? | Yes. He says the principle still applies to scarce life-and-death resources, even if modern Western food scarcity is not the usual issue. | He argues that the old food-scarcity setting is not the main Western example now, but the moral principle still applies wherever a scarce resource can preserve life. He gives antibiotics, pain medication, and organ transplants as examples where taking what one does not need may deprive someone else who does need it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=1504" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:04</a> | Is gluttony only sinful in scarcity situations, or is overeating part of it? | He distinguishes overeating from gluttony, saying biblical gluttony is taking more than one's share in a way that harms others. | He says overeating by itself is not the biblical issue, noting that Israel had feast days that ritualized abundant eating and drinking. Gluttony, in his definition, is excess in a context where another person may be harmed or die because someone took more than a fair share. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=1640" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:20</a> | Does Matthew 16:18 prove the papacy? | He says no; he argues the Greek distinction between Petros and Petra does not support that reading, and Catholics would more naturally appeal to tradition. | He argues that the received Greek text distinguishes Peter as Petros from the Petra on which the church is built. In his reading, the foundation is Christ or the gospel, and Roman Catholic theology would be better served by appealing to church tradition than by making this verse carry the papal argument. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=2022" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:42</a> | How do you correct someone who rejects all scholarship as man-made doctrine? | He says you probably will not break through if the person rejects every tool of learning; even an English Bible already depends on scholarship. | He says that someone rejecting all scholarship often also fails to see that printed Bibles, translations, and reconstructed Greek or Hebrew texts already depend on scholarly work. If the person refuses even that basic point, he advises prayer and moving on rather than expecting the discussion to gain traction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=2407" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:07</a> | Did Moses's Egyptian name originally include a divine element that was later omitted? | He says that is possible; Egyptian names often had theophoric elements, and such elements could be shortened or dropped. | He says many Egyptian names of this type include a divine element, and names can be shortened for several reasons. He thinks Moses probably once had such an element, though he qualifies that some forms may occur without one and that the exact omitted deity cannot be known from the surviving biblical form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=2694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:54</a> | How can someone write a scholarly paper on a subject where little has already been written? | He says those can be good topics, but the writer may need to lay groundwork carefully, publish related foundation work, and stay open to being wrong. | He says neglected topics can be the best research topics, but a paper may need supporting articles, a dissertation, or other groundwork before the main claim is credible. He warns the writer to check whether the topic was ignored, hidden in obscure literature, made toxic by bad work, or already discredited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=3066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:06</a> | How much would someone have to donate for a historical critique of Assassin's Creed Origins? | He jokes about setting a high price, then explains that archaeological games feel like work rather than relaxation now. | He jokes through several possible prices and settles on a high amount because the review would take serious time away from other projects. The reason is not only money: since becoming an archaeologist, playing archaeology-themed games feels like work rather than recreation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=3304" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">55:04</a> | How much do we know about the ancient Egyptian healthcare system, especially care for citizens or the elderly? | He says we know a fair amount: physicians were private practitioners paid in goods, treated the elderly, and provided acute, chronic, surgical, and palliative care. | He points to sources such as the Edwin Smith medical papyrus and describes Egyptian medicine as a mix of medicines, surgery, and incantations. Physicians worked as private practitioners in a barter economy, could be paid in valuables, treated the elderly, used pain relief, performed procedures, and were valued enough to be sent abroad. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=3507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:27</a> | Did ancient Egyptians euthanize people? | He says there is no evidence that they practiced euthanasia, but there is evidence for palliative care and other medical treatment. | He says the evidence points to palliative care rather than euthanasia. Egyptians treated pain, used analgesics and narcotics, splinted bones, cut out tumors, and performed surgical procedures, but he says there is no evidence for euthanasia, abortion, or infanticide in Egypt. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=3791" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:03:11</a> | Do marriages have to be instigated by a priest or pastor? | No. He says Scripture does not require a pastor or priest to conduct the ceremony, even though clergy often represent God's blessing in church tradition. | He says Scripture does not define a required officiant, even though Jewish and Christian traditions commonly use clergy to represent divine blessing. His practical standard is that once a society or religious institution deems a marriage valid, and the parties are free and able to marry, it is a marriage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=4107" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:27</a> | What bad theories get unwarranted promotion because of perceived market value or public image? | He names ancient-alien pyramid claims and flat-earth arguments as examples of fringe ideas that get far more cultural traction than they deserve. | He says claims about aliens building the pyramids have taken up far more public attention than the evidence deserves, even affecting students' assumptions about ancient Egypt. He also mentions flat-earth claims and other fringe archaeology as examples of bad substitutes for real information spreading through the internet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=4566" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:16:06</a> | Why does the Bible not mention the Anatolian Hittite Empire? | He says the empire had already collapsed by the time Israel entered the land, so the Bible encounters later Neo-Hittite states instead. | He says the Anatolian empire was already falling apart around the late Ramesside period and was no longer a world power when Israel entered the land. Biblical references to Hittites, especially in Kings, are better understood as references to Neo-Hittite states rather than the old imperial structure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=5220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:27:00</a> | Is there value in Manetho? | Yes, but cautiously. He says Manetho is useful while also being late and preserved only through later writers whose copies often disagree. | He says Manetho is valuable as one source among many, especially when reign lengths can be compared with other evidence. The caution is that no direct Manetho manuscript survives, the material is preserved through later Jewish and Christian writers, and the copies disagree on important matters such as the Exodus pharaoh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=5491" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:31</a> | Did prophets speak in tongues when communicating with God or angelic beings? | He says the text does not tell us; prophets were brought into God's presence, where the specific language is not the main issue. | He says the biblical text does not identify the language used in prophetic encounters, though a prophet's native language such as Hebrew is plausible. What matters in his answer is that prophets are brought into God's presence or God comes to them, making the specific language secondary and unstated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=5585" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:05</a> | Are the ten kings in Revelation 17:12 the ten Roman provincial governors under Nero? | He is skeptical of that preterist identification because the passage says the kings receive authority with the beast, not merely before Nero. | He rejects the Nero-governor proposal as speculative and says Revelation's ten horns receive authority with the beast, which does not fit dead emperors or past governors. He argues Revelation should be read through Old Testament symbols rather than through a framework that forces the passage into Nero's reign. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=6502" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:22</a> | What is the best biblical argument against Mormonism? | He says the Bible itself is the standard, and later claimed scriptures must accord with the Old and New Testaments; he views the Book of Abraham as especially problematic. | He says any later text claiming scriptural status must fully agree with the Old and New Testaments. On that basis he sees the Book of Mormon, Pearl of Great Price, Doctrine and Covenants, and especially the Book of Abraham as vulnerable, while noting that he is answering from an explicitly Christian standard. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=6693" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:33</a> | What does he think of a proposed reading of Exodus 17:8-16 in light of Heliopolitan cosmology? | He says it is an interesting possibility, but he would need to read the article before judging because some proposed Egyptian connections can be tenuous. | He reads the passage and says Moses' raised hands could be interpreted as a symbolic or parabolic act, but he does not commit to the Heliopolitan connection. He wants to read the article first because proposed Egyptian parallels can be interesting but also tenuous. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=6983" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:23</a> | If he reviewed Assassin's Creed Origins, would it be in a let's-play format? | He says live gameplay might be possible, but screenshots could be more useful for focused critique and publisher asset rules might matter. | He says a recorded playthrough with live reactions could work, but screenshots may let him stop and focus on details more effectively. He also notes that publisher rules about showing game assets could matter, and that his habit of pausing at inscriptions would make gameplay very slow. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=7154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:14</a> | Why is the Mount Ebal curse tablet rejected, and is it valid in any case? | He says the problems are dating and epigraphy: the scans did not clearly show the claimed writing, so calling it a curse tablet is not secure. | He says the object came from a fill pile, so its archaeological context cannot securely date it, and he disputes the ceramic argument used to align it with an early Exodus date. More importantly, he says the tomographic scans do not clearly show an inscription, and without a curse text it cannot be established as a curse amulet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=8205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:16:45</a> | What would you recommend for someone seeking formal biblical education while working and raising children? | He says formal schooling is still better than self-teaching for a formal credential, but family obligations remain the priority and narrow what is realistic. | He distinguishes informal learning from formal education and says formal study forces students to learn topics and languages they would not choose on their own. For someone with work and children, he recommends part-time in-person study where possible, especially because Old and New Testament studies require serious classroom language training. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=8857" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:27:37</a> | Is there evidence outside the Bible that the apostles were martyred? | He says almost all of the evidence is extra-biblical, found in church histories and other later sources rather than in Acts itself. | He says Acts and the rest of the Bible say relatively little about most apostolic martyrdoms because much of that happened after the biblical narrative. The relevant evidence is mostly in church histories, church fathers, and some other later sources, including material about James the brother of Jesus. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=9643" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:43</a> | What do you think of R. C. Sproul's work? | He calls Sproul a strong Reformed teacher, especially on Reformation and New Testament topics, but says his Old Testament handling had weaknesses. | He describes Sproul as well educated, a strong communicator, and much better than average among public teachers. His reservation is that Sproul's Reformation and Calvinist strengths did not translate as well into Old Testament interpretation, where he thinks one must start with the original context before moving to christological significance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=10079" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:59</a> | If it was not a rib that God took from Adam, what was it? | He says the Hebrew term has a broad range around "side" or "rib," but in Genesis 2:22 he thinks the context still points to a rib-like element rather than a whole side. | He says the Hebrew term has a wide semantic range and can refer to side or rib depending on context. Genesis 2:21 speaks of taking one from Adam's side, while verse 22 describes that item being fashioned into the woman, which leads him to favor a rib-like reading in this context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=10613" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:53</a> | Why does Genesis say woman was made from a rib, and is there relevant ancient context? | He suggests there may be a cultic or sacred-flesh reason, but he is not sure and says he would need more ancient Near Eastern evidence before being definite. | He connects the passage to Genesis' sacred-space and image-bearing themes and speculates that Adam's sanctified role could explain why the woman is made from the same flesh. He is careful not to settle the point, saying he has not yet found enough ancient Near Eastern material to make the interpretation firm. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=10917" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:57</a> | What is your view of E. A. Wallis Budge's work as an Egyptologist? | He says Budge popularized Egyptian material and produced impressive editions, but his translations were weak even by standards of his own time. | He says Budge had money, published attractive and accessible editions, and helped popularize Egyptian texts for English readers. The problem is that he was self-taught, weak at hieroglyphs, and produced translations and grammars that were faulty even compared with scholars working in his own era. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=11418" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:10:18</a> | What do you think of Budge's other works, such as translations of New Testament apocrypha and legendary texts? | He says Budge's Greek or Latin work may fare better, but his output varies because he sometimes translated with only cursory knowledge. | He says Budge's background in classical education means Greek or Latin translations may be better than his Egyptian work, but he still warns that Budge's range exceeded his competence. His other translations vary because he sometimes worked from only a shallow knowledge of the language involved. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=11755" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:15:55</a> | Who counts as a legitimate Egyptologist? | He says the standard depends on era, but today a PhD in Egyptology and recognition by other Egyptologists are the usual marks. | He says older eras sometimes recognized people with a master's degree or other serious training if they were doing legitimate work in the field. Today, he identifies the usual standard as a PhD in Egyptology proper plus recognition from other Egyptologists, such as the recommendations required for the International Association of Egyptologists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=11980" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:40</a> | Was James Henry Breasted a good Egyptologist, and are his Ancient Records volumes worth using? | He says Breasted was a competent Egyptologist for his time, but the translations are badly dated and are now freely available; he also sharply criticizes Breasted's ethics. | He credits Breasted as a competent Egyptologist and major figure behind the Oriental Institute collection, while noting that the Ancient Records translations date to 1906 and are now far out of date. He also strongly criticizes Breasted's eugenics and racial views, separating technical competence from moral assessment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=12191" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:23:11</a> | What were ancient Israelite family units like, and how would they view modern nursing homes and pushing kids out at 18? | He says Israelite clan life was multigenerational and wealth-preserving, so they would have seen that modern pattern as both cruel and wasteful. | He describes rural Israelite clan life as multigenerational, village-based, and organized around mutual protection, shared labor, and inherited wealth. Children were helped into stable households nearby, and elderly parents were reintegrated into family care, so he says modern isolation of young adults and elders would look both cruel and economically foolish. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=12572" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:32</a> | What language would Abraham and the Egyptians have communicated in, and did Egyptians use interpreters? | He says Abraham likely spoke Amorite, while Egyptian scribes and interpreters handled foreign-language diplomacy and correspondence. | He says Abraham's native language was probably Amorite. Egyptians did use interpreters, and language work belonged to the scribal class, whose advanced training could include languages needed for diplomacy, correspondence, and interaction with foreign courts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=12705" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:31:45</a> | What are your biggest problems with evolution? | He mentions concerns about circular dating claims and the difficulty of some proposed macroevolutionary sequences, while not adopting a standard creationist label. | He lists concerns about circular reasoning in dating strata and fossils, the statistical difficulty of some macroevolutionary sequences, and the instability of evolutionary models over time. He also stresses that this is not his field and that he is not trying to attach himself to a moving theory. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=13147" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:07</a> | Did any early church fathers believe God's throne is metaphorical? | He is not sure offhand, but says the church fathers held a wide range of views and that an allegorical writer such as Origen would not surprise him. | He cannot name an example from memory, but he warns against treating the church fathers as a single unified voice. Because early Christian writers used a wide range of literal and allegorical readings, he says it would not surprise him if an allegorical interpreter held such a view. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=13292" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:41:32</a> | Is there any ancient Egyptian record of mistranslated diplomatic communication used to damage relations? | He says no; diplomacy was dangerous, messages were handled by court scribes, and deliberate mistranslation would have carried severe consequences. | He says diplomacy in the ancient Near East was dangerous enough that deliberately corrupting a message would be reckless. Messages were delivered to the receiving court's scribes for translation, and either foreign diplomats or local scribes could face severe punishment if they endangered diplomatic relations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=13778" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:38</a> | What are your biggest disagreements with the church? | He says his main disagreements are usually with denominational distinctives and with pastors protecting people from Scripture rather than teaching the whole Bible. | He says his disagreements are less with the universal church's core doctrines and more with denominational distinctives and weak leadership. He especially objects to pastors shielding people from difficult biblical material, because that produces biblical ignorance and stronger attachment to secondary issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=14086" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:54:46</a> | What does he mean by "fundamentalist"? | He describes fundamentalism as a branch of Christianity marked by a wooden-literal reading, separation doctrines, often dispensationalism, and strong attachment to certain secondary distinctives. | He describes fundamentalism as a branch of Christianity that often stresses a wooden literalism, secondary separation, pre-tribulation rapture teaching, dispensationalism, and isolation from other groups. He acknowledges that some core beliefs are sound, but says the secondary distinctives often become over-weighted. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=15554" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:19:14</a> | When should someone leave a church? | His answer is that you leave when God calls you elsewhere, and that can include leaving either a bad church or a good one for ministry reasons. | He says the basic answer is to leave when God calls you elsewhere, which can include mission work, relocation, or other service even if the church is good. Bad reasons to stay include churches encouraging sin, embracing heresy, or lacking the spiritual practices needed to feed the congregation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=15962" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:26:02</a> | If a church is bad, should someone leave or try to change it? | He says one should first try to change it, but many bad churches resist correction, so the answer requires prayer and discernment. | He says the first impulse should be to try to change the church, but many unhealthy churches are resistant because the leadership and congregation often share the same posture. Deciding whether to stay, leave, or take a break requires prayer, discernment, and attention to whether God is calling the person elsewhere. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=16042" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:27:22</a> | What if everything is good at a church, but the preacher mainly paraphrases Scripture? | He calls that "expository light" and says it may not be an immediate reason to leave, but it often leads a church toward mediocrity over time. | He distinguishes true expository preaching, which reads, explains, and applies the passage in detail, from topical preaching and light paraphrase. A church may not need to be abandoned immediately over this, but he says shallow treatment of the text often limits the church's long-term health. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=16342" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:32:22</a> | What advice would you give people who want to become pastors, especially about sermon preparation? | He emphasizes language study, seminary preparation, and serious weekly work in the text rather than shallow paraphrase. | He says pastors need strong seminary preparation, especially in Greek and Hebrew, because sermon quality depends on prior training. He describes sermon preparation as 15 to 20 hours of work, mostly spent in the original languages and commentaries before writing and rehearsing the sermon. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=16799" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:39:59</a> | Why study biblical languages if teachers or translators may put their own tilt on them? | He says learning the languages is precisely how one evaluates translation choices and checks whether a tilt is present. | He says an English translation is already an interpretive opinion, so knowing the language is how someone can test whether a translation is right. Greek and Hebrew also open related literature and reveal ambiguity, grammar, and turns of phrase that cannot always be reproduced in English. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=17818" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:56:58</a> | Should churches use others for grunt work so pastors can study? | Yes. He says that is the function of deacons, drawing on the apostles' decision to devote themselves to prayer and Scripture rather than serving tables. | He says churches should protect the pastor's study time by using deacons and others for practical service work. He roots that in the apostolic pattern of appointing others to serve tables so the apostles could devote themselves to prayer and Scripture. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=18075" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:01:15</a> | What do you think of Constantine the Great? | He says too much is made of Constantine: he was a Roman politician, not a pillar of faith, and his role at Nicaea is often overstated. | He portrays Constantine primarily as a Roman emperor and politician, neither the great monster nor the saint of popular portrayals. He says Constantine wanted the Arian controversy resolved at Nicaea, but his role was limited, and his late baptism fits a common Roman pattern of postponing baptism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/fVPZXw6Vbt0?t=18336" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:05:36</a> | If someone cannot go to school, how should they learn Hebrew and Greek? | He says first-year Greek can be self-taught with good resources, but Hebrew and advanced Greek really require formal courses. | He says first-year Greek can be self-taught with a good introductory text, though that may make later study harder. Hebrew, second-year Greek, and third-year Greek are different: he strongly recommends formal classes because the languages are difficult and shallow study can lead to confident but wrong exegesis. |
